content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 6, 2005

John Pickard
4087 Hare Avenue
Riverside, CA 92509

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. W-08-169

Dear Mr. Pickard:

This letter is in response to your request for advice, written on September 23, 2008. As we discussed on October 3, 2008, your inquiry presents issues which are outside the purview of the Political Reform Act ("Act")¹.

For example, you ask whether it is legal for Southern California Edison to use ratepayer money to pay for its political campaign. While the Act constrains contribution limits to candidates and reporting of contributions to ballot measures, we do not regulate the use of ratepayer funds by public utilities. This is outside the Fair Political Practices Commission's (the "Commission") statutory authority. However, included below and enclosed please find general resources that may assist you in resolving this matter.

• For your question regarding Southern California Edison, you may wish to contact the California Public Utilities Commission (CPUC). The CPUC can be reached by calling (213) 576-7000 or visiting their website at:

http://www.cpuc.ca.gov/PUC/contactus/.

• For your questions concerning the League of California Cities and AARP, you may want to contact those organizations for the information. You also may want to contact the Internal Revenue Service which regulates the political activity of nonprofit entities.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. W-08-169
Page No. 2

http://www.irs.gov/charities/nonprofits/index.html

• Alternatively, there are public and private resources to track how ballot measure campaigns are financed. The California Secretary of State provides an online database of all contributions made to and by state ballot measures and candidates, and the National Institute on Money in State Politics is a nonpartisan, nonprofit organization that tracks the influence of campaign money on state-level politics. You may access these, respectively, by visiting:

http://www.sos.ca.gov/.

www.followthemoney.org

Finally, enclosed please find a copy of the Act, which explains the jurisdiction of the FPPC. Should you need assistance in the future, you may contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallibrin
General Counsel

Amber Maltbie
Legal Intern, Legal Division
AM:jgl

Enclosures

sections.question_synthetic: The requestor asked whether it is legal for Southern California Edison to use ratepayer money to pay for political campaigns, and raised related questions about the League of California Cities and AARP's political activities.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC lacks statutory authority to address these questions because they fall outside the scope of the Political Reform Act. The Commission regulates contribution limits and ballot measure reporting, but does not regulate utility ratepayer fund usage or nonprofit political activity, which are under the jurisdiction of the CPUC and IRS respectively.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a declination letter without traditional QUESTION and CONCLUSION sections. The legal question and FPPC's position are embedded in the body text. File number shows 'W-08-169' indicating withdrawal/declination. Minor OCR artifacts noted in header ('PRRCTTCFS' and formatting inconsistencies).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The requestor asked whether it is legal for Southern California Edison to use ratepayer money to pay for political campaigns, and raised related questions about the League of California Cities and AARP's political activities.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC lacks statutory authority to address these questions because they fall outside the scope of the Political Reform Act. The Commission regulates contribution limits and ballot measure reporting, but does not regulate utility ratepayer fund usage or nonprofit political activity, which are under the jurisdiction of the CPUC and IRS respectively.

embedding.summary: The FPPC declines to provide advice on whether Southern California Edison may use ratepayer funds for political campaigns, determining the matter falls outside its statutory authority under the Political Reform Act. The letter directs the requestor to the CPUC, IRS, and other resources for resolution.
